title: The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
subtitle: The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.
caption:
The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles

The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.

The line chart tracks the gender wage ratio in the United States from 1980 to 2010 across three distinct income percentiles: the tenth, fiftieth, and ninetieth. All three series display an upward trajectory over the thirty-year period, indicating a narrowing of the wage gap. In 1980, the ratios for all three groups are clustered between 60% and 65%. The line representing the tenth percentile begins at approximately 65%, rises steadily to roughly 75% by 1990, and continues to climb until it converges with the fiftieth percentile line near the 80% mark around 1998. The fiftieth percentile line starts just above 60% in 1980 and follows a consistent upward path, finishing slightly above 80% in 2010. The ninetieth percentile line begins near 62% and rises to approximately 73% by 1990. Unlike the lower percentiles, this series flattens significantly after 1990, maintaining a level between 73% and 75% through 2010. By the end of the period, the tenth and fiftieth percentiles have reached similar values above 80%, while the ninetieth percentile remains distinctively lower, hovering in the mid-70s range.
